author: @miken7629
text: The pumps of Saturn 5 rocket engines were boat propeller blades which are designed to move liquid.  It would be interesting to build a pump using toroidal propellers and see if high(er) pressure could be achieved.  All conventional liquid fuel rocket engines are based on pressure thrust so more head pressure could produce more powerful engines.

author: @GORT70
text: No. The wind turbines cannot be made reliable. It’s that simple.

author: @drprepper4393
text:
Regular airplane props to toroidal is a pretty big leap. I know it sounds so simple that someone probably a lot of folk, has already done this but I can’t find evidence of or anyone who has tried putting simple winglets on normal, regular airplane propellers. If they work on wind turbines increasing efficiency 4-5% then…? Yes, one is pushed by wind & the other propels it. But isn’t it to simple to overlook? Seems like ANY improvement would justify a cheap screw on “Neglets”* kit. Right?! Or tested on helicopter blades or even part of your myriad & diverse test propellers. Seriously, how hard could it be to simply add winglets to normally produced blades and propellers be they for air or water. 
 *”Neglets” Negating Vortices Winglets. To simply to patent. Let’s just make them open source/public domain. SemperFi

author: @boogieknee3781
text:
Rather than print the blade......on an expensive toy......
cut a plastic bottle into ribbons along its curve,sure this was tedious but by examining the geometry of the twist itself you can then attach it to your axle(old cotton reel was a good starting point)which is easy enough to make consistently once you know the 3d shape you prefer.
fyi....by leaving tabs at either end of the ribbon I was able to make better joints by pushing the tabs through slits in the axles.

author: @TheWayneStevenson
text:
Better is entirely subjective I would think. The excitement on this design that has been catching winds, has been the benefits in noise reduction. Not in efficiency. With that said, there have been years of noise complaints from people living near wind farms. It's entirely possible that this propeller design, while used in high-RPM thrust driving applications (drones), will also have noise reduction benefits at lower speeds.

Now that's a comparison I would love to hear. ;)

Nice channel , always a pleasure to watch 🇬🇧👍

author: @Spiritman597
text: I think there's a mis on that new blade. Why did the new blade not cover all the available area. It only had 2 loops. Those loops did not cover all the available area. Your old blade had 3 find but the new blade only had 2 loops. Also part of the claim on the new blade is supposed to be more stable and less noise. I've seen them on several drones that seem to increase the manuverability

author: @Richibald1
text: Yes aircraft TOROIDAL propellors have been demonstrated to be quieter and faster, therefore more* efficient. I see no reason why TOROIDAL wind turbines can't be made more efficient with properly designed TOROIDAL blades.

author: @williammaxwell1919
text: Post WW2 wind turbine blades were purportedly based on Mk1 Spitfire 3-blade propellers. By the end of the war, Mk16 had a 6 blade prop. Many modern turbo-props have 6 or more blades  and bladelets to the tip of the blade plus surface profiling to reduce drag, trailing edge fluting, etc.... and yet nearly 80 years of propeller inovation and evolution seems to have escaped the wind power sector.... there is sooo much science that is not being applied to this energy sector ... I wonder why?

author: @lachlanraidal5100

author: @baladar1353
text:
Hey Robert,
I have an interesting notice over the notifications of the videos.
You uploaded this particular video to the site three days ago. However, youtube (...) didn't bother itself to notice me about it then. It waited three days, and sent the notification about your "new" video only four hours ago. Strange, because I have subscribed to your channel more than a year ago. I remember subscribing, so don't understand why those "good intended" algorhythms forgot to notice me about it. Actually they unsubscribed me from your channel, without letting me know about their will and without my consent. I have had some uploaded content, which have disappeared without a trace maybe two years ago. Of course the advertisements always came up when wanted to watch something, so it must be done by purpose. I think they don't want me (and many others) to watch your videos, but I don't get why. You're not literally against the system, but not the low-minded average idiotic "content maker" they prefer. Anyway, I wanted to write it down, maybe the good intention of theirs won't make it disappear before you read it.

author: @geralltgriffiths7772
text: That's a bit perfunctory for you, Rob. I'd wager that there will be some conditions (e.g. low or excessively high wind) or characteristics (noise, reliability, performance range, etc) where "horses for courses" applies. While some ideas eventually prove to be flash-in-the-pan fads, dismissing them on the basis of a single test is likely to stifle innovation.

author: @dinky9216
text:
When used with a motor they produce less turbulence. I wouldn't expect much of a difference for a single turbine as you wouldn't normally care about the wind shadow. However, if you had more than one, I suspect you could place them closer together in a wind farm.

Conversely, I wonder if they are more  tolerant of turbulent air? If that is ever a concern?

author: @Gainn
text: What's the weight difference?

author: @kadmow
text:
- yes, yes, but open circuit voltage demonstrates little (real power is bankable) - for a wind turbine power vs drag force is one metric (ie how much material is needed to support the device in a gale force wind... Appreciate you are not new to this game (giving folk a laugh and a lesson at the same time), just saying - cheers.

*yes I take a look  (esp the intersecting clover leaf design doing the rounds) and see aerodynamic red flags. (not least of which is blade interference)  
For eliminating tip vortices - the old school string pull propeller toy with the tip-ring, looks a good non optimal place to start.. lol. ie. see if a pre-"drone" toy can beat "MIT" for a laugh, how much of a ring (or part thereof) benefits the system.

author: @yohell
text: I hear the main benefit of toroidal propellers is that they are supposed to cause less high frequency noise. Cool if they can do that while still being about as efficient in power production. But I suppose that's only evident at very high rpms, like drone rotors.

author: @bukszpryt_
text:
From what i've seen in some video about this type of propeller, it is supposed to create less turbulences because there are no blade tips. It's supposed to be a bit more quiet (at least in some frequency range) and wear slower than regular blade.
So it's not about better efficiency in converting power to wind or vice versa, but about making it less annoying.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Good demo.  I think you may have covered the general idea long ago with your paper pinwheel, which had the advantage of taking energy from off-axis.  It may be a "hot topic" only because the manufacturer that makes props for boats with the design is in a heavy promotion and investment phase, and trying to expand. Unlike your old pinwheel, their advantage seems to be in an air or water screw drive performance - rather than as a driven turbine blade.

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text:
Yup, less turbulences in water as a propeller - less cavitation and less noise pollution.
Slightly worse performance as a turbine blade for air turbines.

author: @Adam.Rushing
text: There were tens of millions of dollars dumped into R&D before the wind energy industry took off several years ago.  The results of that research we are able to see on the huge windmills that have been fairly standardized and erected - the three blade design is the most efficient.  That was all the info I needed!

replies.author: @misamsung6191
replies.text: IDK, the first trains were steam with open carriages.  But they evolved into trains like the TGV in France or the Japanese Shinkansen trains.  The same can be said for cars and aeroplanes.  Just because it works doesn't mean that it can not be made better.

replies.author: @Adam.Rushing
@misamsung6191
replies.text:
@Adam.Rushing Things evolve as materials and techniques improve.  Even the blades have evolved over time and it has very little to do with test equipment and wind tunnels are pretty much the same as they've always have been.  What has changed is the test equipment has evolved.

You started this thread with the statement, "The results of that research we are able to see on the huge windmills that have been fairly standardized and erected - the three blade design is the most efficient.  That was all the info I needed!"  Windmills have been used to mill grain, and pump water for centuries and they come in a variety of shapes and sizes.  The current design does have limitations and other designs have been proposed.  This one is a combination of efficiency and cost.

replies.author: @ScandalistRick
replies.text:
That is a good point, but I feel some could just as easily argue that would be too much of an advantage for the toroidal.  

Technically that toroidal fan has 4 working blades.  Albeit they are a bit thinner and have less surface area than the tri-blade fan, but that is made up by having that 'extra' blade.  With that said, I'd presume they have almost the same 'total blade surface area' when compared to each other.  I feel Robert did very well in choosing these two.

I feel a way to get even more accurate would be to create a 4 toroid fan, and put it against an 8-blade fan.  I would make them so that the 'center mass' of each is identical, then I would change the size of each blade for the non-toroidal (constant thickness AND length/sweep, variable width) until both fans weight exactly the same.  This *should* ensure a very close blade surface area between the two.

author: @yougeo
text: I do see one problem with your method. Your fan is shooting a very narrow column of air which is not hitting the tips of the different propellers. It is the suppression of the tip flow of a standard propeller blade that a toroidal blade gains efficiency from. So if your air column doesn't encompass the whole blade including the tips then you're not likely to see the difference if there is a difference. Underwater tests with ink dye have shown that there is a suppression of the Eddie's that occur at the tip of a typical propeller when you instead use a toroidal propeller. And of course any remaining eddies in water or air are uncaptured energy. The ideal propeller would leave the air flow exactly as it was when it entered the propeller but it a lesser speed because it extracted energy perfectly from the air flow and anything that it couldn't extract remained in nearly laminar flow so there was no wasted or leftover energy.

author: @DerekRoss1958
text: Graphs comparing the two types at different speeds seem to show minimal differences in power or noise at low speeds. It looks like you need to get up to 3,000 rpm or more before you start to see any difference. While you can get those sorts of speeds with a motor-driven propeller, you're unlikely to get that fast with a wind- or water-driven turbine. So maybe toroidals are beneficial for propulsion but they are unlikely to have any benefit for power generation. As Rob said in the video!

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
To really understand a turbine's performance compared to others you need to compare not so much the voltage, but to compare their power output. Mater of fact, the Betz limit is all about power output/power in the wind, and what the limit is at different tip speed ratios. The Betz limit says nothing about voltage. We simply only need voltage along with resistance in ohms to determin power.  A given turbine's efficency is a function of its tip speed ratio and its tip speed ratio is going to depend on how much the turbine is loaded.

Please take 5 minutes to google coefficient of power, tip speed ratio and look for the graphs that include the savinouse, American Multiblade, High Speed Two Blade, and Darius turbines, and study the graph to visualize the relationships. 

I put a lot of effort into being constructive, especially if im going to be critical. I love what your doing and understand keeping things simple, and maybe im too technical, but sometimes too simple really misses the mark.  This video was almost useless for comparing two turbines. 

Try this at least once. Make yourself a potentiometer with some of your conductive ink. Mark out different resistances by position. Then take voltage readings across the circuit resistances with current from the turbine and generator, and graph both voltage and power along the Y axis against resistance on the X axis, with power calculated by watts=voltage²/resistance. You will discover how you load a turbine strongly influences how well it performs.

Cavitation is not a problem in air like it is in water so maybe toroidal blades are best suited to boat propellors?

author: @TheNiteinjail
text:
I think the exciting bit about toroidal 'blades' is not that it offers staggering advantages any one place ... but it offers an entirely new geometry to work with to tweak and get particular traits where it's needed... like very quiet for instance.   They won't be a panacea that makes all props and turbines better everywhere under all cases... it's just like someone has moved the creativity barrier a bit farther back and now more options are possible. Will they be even better ducted? how about 3 or 4 loops?

Also ... not much chance you'd nail the perfect geometry in one go. lol  It took decades, big brains, computers and a lot of wind tunnel time to tweak out the most efficient props and blades for a million different uses... The same will happen at blazing speed with toroidal loops.

author: @snekmeseht
text: When they design wind turbines, they must keep blade tip speed below, for example, 60m/s to meet noise requirements. It's about vortex shedding off the tip. This means they either have to limit rpm or swept diameter. The claim for the vortex is that since the tip is spread over a much larger span, it will run quieter. That would mean the wind turbine blade could be bigger in swept diameter or the wind turbine could spin the blade faster and still keep the noise down.

author: @Silent_Flight
text:
The 8 blade is supposed to be more efficient with a reduction in noise for the same thrust.

The primary research came from research into making ships screws more efficient.

A general rule of thumb in aviation is that unless it's a fan, 1 blade can work if counter balanced, 2 is very noisy (unless it's thick and slow - e.g. WW1 rotary engines) for a bit more thrust, 3 is optimal, 4 starts to loose thrust but is quieter.

author: @actudoran
text:
they've been designed and optimised for wing tip vortex reduction in the sense that the shocks created by the wingtip of a standard prop get to dissipate along the entire curved surface hence more time for it to be absorbed by the airmass surrounding the device.

there are multiple variants of it and I do believe that there is some benefit to using it as an active propeller at high RPM in that it's less noisy.... as for efficiency it's probably going to be subpar at top rpm but more effective in midrange rpm ... top at 3k, mid at about 1.5k
that's based on the paper I've seen published and their graphic interpretation of data.

given your experiment, probably more blades on the std prop would be far more beneficial than a toroidal prop

replies.author: @kodfkdleepd2876
replies.text:
@actudoran If you can't afford one then you can still work on making the geometry... then put them up on a free sharing site or get in some group and someone will definitely print them for you for costs only(shipping, material). E.g., you could make 50 versions and then send a person(or online site) the cad files and they will print them and send them back to you.

You might even be able to find someone who will do it for free. You could call up some university's fluid dynamics or robotics department and show them your ideas and they might do it and send them to you for free(of course nothing is free in life).

You could, if you really wanted to pursue it: Use AI to generate the optimal geometry for you. This is actually easier than you think. AI is just a giant curve fitting "machine"(its like fitting a line to points using least squares but it's in much higher dimensions(millions or more usually)).

There is a mathematical algorithm called differential evolution. Things they do with this is find the optimal of geometric structures according to some conditions(it's like a solver for physical problems that returns the best geometry for X). An example is that of designing a stator design for a motor.  You can give a basic geometry description that is general enough as to parameterize "stator geometry space" for all basic designs. E.g. it's basic design starts off with an annulus which is each to describe mathematically with just an inner and our radius(r1,r2). You do this for all the parts of the thing you are modeling and end up with a bunch of "tweaking" parameters that you can adjust to get different basic stator designs)... so in this case it's like photoshop for stators except instead of adjusting RGB's, contrasts, etc, you are adjusting length, thicknesses, distances, material properties, etc. Then you just setup the constraints which are represented by constraints on the parameters. (e.g., r1 > 0, r2 > r1, etc)

Then DE will find the optimal geometry within the "basic stators". DE is sorta like genetic programming and simple optimization where it uses collections of sample points to sample space in a very particular pattern which works well for quickly finding the local extrema(the math is no prone to certain types of errors that ruin more basic optimization algorithms)). It acts like genetics in that it uses mutations crossover, etc to blend "the best of the best" to find the global optimum. 

If you can follow what I'm saying, is that you can use the same idea to optimize for the design and get the best geometry under the space of airfoils that you've generalized to(your parameter or phase space). E.g., if you allow the ability to have multiple blades so your function that generates the geometry can take an argument to set the n blades then DE will find the optimal geometry for the blades *GIVEN* the limitations of the "airfoil space" you've created and the conditions/requirements/constraints you've set up on the parameters.

So basically you get to design your "airfoil space" which we will call A and have it parameterized by pj parameters. This is just a function like AirFoil(p1,p2,p3,....) which returns the geometry. (essentially is just an indicator function that says which points in space are part of the airfoil and which are not).

Then you have to apply fluid dynamics or somehow get the "performance"(the value to be optimized) of an airfoil that was generated.

Performance(AirFoil(p1,...))

Then you have constraints which are just logic equations that let's one limit the parameters as to give a correct mapping(the space we are optimizing over may not be Euclidean).

DE then, when everything is setup up right, will return given enough time, the global optimal.

AI is essentially the same process except you would have to simulate to get the performance a much denser phase space to feed in to a NN to get it to learn the dynamics.

There is also SINDY which allows you to reverse the dynamics in to differential equations. but here too you need a lot of data.

So you don't really need a 3D printer right now, you can just start designing the algorithm to generate your 3D airfoil model and then either use it to print cad models which you can then either have a 3D printer by then or have found someone willing to print them up for you for free.

So you could do it if you really wanted. It might find some strange solutions that you never thought before. AI can be quite a bit more general and find some solutions that may not be obvious.

text: How do the 2 blades compare under various wind velocities? Is one better than the other at very low velocities? How do the 2 blades compare at varying angles of incidence of wind to the blade? All we've seen is wind square on  at high velocity. This is far from a comprehensive examination of the properties of the 2 blades.

replies.author: @TheChzoronzon
replies.text:
In actual flights, installed in a drone, the loss of efficiency is huge... like a 25% of a classic blade propeller. Just put "toroidal propeller" in the Search bar.

Another flask of snake oil, it seems

author: @AncientTechDIYChannel
text: the perfect propeller design was already made in the 1930s.......

author: @cayrex
text: Nice video demostration. Several days ago I see the video about that propeller from MIT. That propeller need also reduce the nois!? What about trying 3 blade toroidal propeller?

replies.author: @cayrex
replies.text: @rocklover7437 Here is also the question if the models of Toroidal Propeller on internet are the same like by the MIT (right angle, etc).

replies.author: @TheChzoronzon
replies.text: In fact, we don't know... the MIT vid (or even the tech paper, lmao!) doesn't mention at all any of the data required to do a fair comparison, it's just marketing poopoo

author: @NM-wd7kx
text: As has been demonstrated a few times now, the improved performance is at very high speeds (IE: drone propellers)

replies.author: @TheChzoronzon
replies.text:
Watch Jays Tech Vault test (or in fact, any actual test like Will It Mod channel, or  Dr Quads etc) and then look the meaning of the word "demonstrated" in a dictionary... hint: it doesn't mean what you think :)

Bottom line: you arguably get some noise improvement at the cost of a huge loss in efficiency.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @TheChzoronzon They are very hard to tweak so any result so far can't be taken seriously, I didn't check the MIT article about it and how they did designed their model.

replies.author: @TheChzoronzon
replies.text:
@vitordelima The MIT "article" is a marketing brochure with zero data so we compare it with classical design and also zero info about the design process...

...but we can imagine: they took an idea that works for boat propellers and with zero rigurosity tried to sell it to drone nerds cause they know the Fry Effect DOES work: "Shut up and take my money!!"

author: @EUPThatsMe
text: Seams like the main advantage to the toroidal propeller is the lack or reduction of vortices and thus noise when driven. Not really a surprise that it didn't work better as a turbine than standard blades.  Although, it would be interesting to try both inside a housing to see how they compare w.r.t. noise.

replies.author: @NM-wd7kx
replies.text: Check out the fan showdown demonstration of it, even at 2,000 rpm (with his own, simple design) it was roughly the same perceived noise as a noctua standard (his base), though definitely a different tone.

replies.author: @Milkybar3320011
replies.text: I could see the noise benefit as a driven fan, but as a wind turbine the wind itself would probably cancel the blade noise.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @NM-wd7kx It wasn't fine tuned for the amount of torque/RPM used there. The ship propellers that inspired this idea also look quite different.

replies.author: @Adam.Rushing
replies.text:
EUP, I dunno...the main advantage of the Toroidal may be that it has considerably more surface area.  
Why you ask?
Because more surface area = more confused birds = more dead birds = more delicious BBQ! :)

replies.author: @NM-wd7kx
replies.text:
@vitordelima true, but I wonder how much the  density:RPM ratio affects things.
If you took one of the boat props (dimensionally), then ran it at a proportionally higher RPM I wonder how it would work out, it'd never be as good as a properly designed one, but it'd be interesting

replies.text: Not in the slightest!! Geez what an unscientific test. How do the 2 blades compare under various wind velocities? Is one better than the other at very low velocities? How do the 2 blades compare at varying angles of incidence of wind to the blade? All we've seen is wind square on  at high velocity. author: @ToninFightsEntropy
text: Lol! That was interesting! I wonder how it'd perform with more blades

replies.author: @donaldhanson2908
replies.text:
exactly, the "wind turbine" had a 3:2 advantage in lift.
in addition to being tuned for lifting rather than propulsion.
two different jobs.

replies.author: @ToninFightsEntropy
replies.text:
@donaldhanson2908 Does that really make a difference to how they'd be shaped??
Though I dunno about a 3:2 advantage here.. the material used for those two blades could easily be more than was used for the 3 on the other.

replies.author: @nybble41
replies.text: @ToninFightsEntropy There is more material, but the toroidal propeller is effectively four blades (connected in pairs at the tips) vs. three blades for the traditional propeller. However, those four blades are spaced unevenly around the axis and probably not optimized for the wind speed used in this test or for power generation rather than propulsion. Their main advantage is supposed to be performance (and noise) at very high RPMs.
